topic: Which framework — Wails or Electron — best fits a cross-platform Kanban board desktop app, weighing footprint & native backend against rendering consistency, ecosystem, and delivery speed?

content.position: Wails is the better fit: single binary 8-15MB, idles 30-60MB RSS vs Electron 120-180MB / 200-400MB. Kanban workload fits native webview; Go backend helps sync/file I/O/board state.
content.conditions: Team comfortable in Go; primarily Windows/macOS; mainstream HTML/CSS; footprint treated as real product quality.
content.wouldChangeIf: Linux first-class with heavy WebKitGTK reliance; team has zero Go fluency; needs mature Electron-specific native-module ecosystem.
content.keyRisk: Per-OS webview inconsistency (esp. WebKitGTK) erases the footprint win via QA cost; smaller ecosystem means hand-building auto-update/packaging.

content.position: Electron. Kanban lives/dies by drag-drop feel; one bundled Chromium = identical dnd-kit drag previews/collision/animation across all 3 OSes; mature React board ecosystem ships fast.
content.conditions: React/TS-fluent team, tight deadline, modern desktops with RAM to spare, light backend.
content.wouldChangeIf: Footprint/RAM is a hard requirement (<50MB installer / constrained hardware), OR a Wails PoC proves dnd-kit-equivalent drag fidelity across all 3 native webviews without per-platform bugs.
content.keyRisk: Chromium per-instance memory + install footprint + ongoing Electron security-hardening cost; skimping on hardening ships an insecure app.

content.framing: The crux is whether one bundled Chromium is LOAD-BEARING for reproducible drag-and-drop fidelity, or merely insurance paid in 100+MB / 200+MB RAM; and whether the Go backend is a real asset or a red herring for this not-CPU-bound workload.
divergencePoints: Reproducibility of dnd-kit drag fidelity across per-OS webviews
Is the Go backend an asset or a non-sequitur
Weighting footprint vs delivery/ecosystem for a non-CPU-bound board
hiddenAgreements: Hard footprint constraint flips toward Wails
App is not CPU-bound; UI is webview web tech
A Wails drag-fidelity PoC is the decisive evidence

content.position: Wails is the right default for footprint and a clean native backend, but I concede drag-and-drop fidelity is genuinely the deciding axis — so the recommendation is conditional on a one-week WebView2/WKWebView/WebKitGTK dnd-kit PoC, not asserted. Lowering confidence 0.62 to 0.55 until that PoC runs.
content.reasoning: Steel-man of r1-msg-002: dnd-kit fidelity bottoms out in pointer-event capture, sub-pixel CSS transform interpolation, rAF timing, and scroll-during-drag — in each platform's compositor/input stack, NOT shared JS. Q-A (honest): cross-webview drag identity is an UNTESTED assumption a PoC must validate. Q-B (honest): the backend is largely orthogonal — the real differentiator is single-binary distribution + a static, GC'd, memory-bounded process + tighter attack surface; so the Go backend is closer to a red herring than an asset for this workload.
proposals: One-week PoC gate across Win11+WebView2 / macOS+WKWebView / Ubuntu+WebKitGTK.
Decision rule: fidelity is the gate, footprint the tiebreaker.
content.wouldChangeIf: A controlled dnd-kit PoC passing across all three webviews pushes me back to Wails at 0.7+; any measurable jank on WebKitGTK/WebView2 flips me to Electron.

references.comment: Steel-manned and largely conceded its drag-fidelity point.
Accept the Go backend is closer to a red herring.
Lowering own 0.62 to 0.55, conditional on the PoC.

content.position: Electron is the safer ship for a drag-heavy, animated Kanban because one bundled Chromium retires an entire class of per-OS webview drag/animation bugs by construction. But I concede footprint is real, and if a Wails PoC reproduces dnd-kit fidelity on WebKitGTK, the residual case for Electron narrows to ecosystem/tooling maturity, not rendering.
content.reasoning: Steel-man r1-msg-001: footprint is not vanity. Concrete drag-failure modes favoring one Chromium: (1) WebKitGTK weaker Pointer Events capture (setPointerCapture loss mid-drag); (2) transform:translate3d DragOverlay layer-promotion/subpixel-snap -> jitter; (3) scroll-during-drag quirks across WKWebView/WebKitGTK; (4) backdrop-filter and (5) position:sticky headers WebKit lagged. If a Wails PoC reproduces fidelity on all three, residual Electron edge = electron-builder/electron-updater signed/notarized/delta auto-update + React ecosystem. Go backend is a red herring unless sync grows CPU-heavy.
proposals: 2-3 day spike with one acceptance test recorded on all 3 webviews.
Decouple drag-parity from the ~1-2 week cost to hand-build signed auto-update.
content.wouldChangeIf: A recorded PoC showing fidelity pixel-identical and jank-free on all three webviews AND Wails signed/notarized/delta auto-update under ~a week flips me to Wails.

references.comment: Steel-manned the footprint win, then countered the WebKitGTK QA risk is the understated load-bearing cost.
Names what survives once drag-parity is retired; concedes Go backend is a red herring.

content.consensusTargeted: The r1-msg-004/r1-msg-005 consensus that a ~1-week tri-webview dnd-kit PoC is the decisive gate: fidelity reproduces -> Wails on footprint; else Electron; Go backend irrelevant; auto-update tooling the only other differentiator.
content.loadBearingAssumption: That a single short-horizon, single-machine PoC is a valid PROXY for the multi-year, multi-machine reality of shipping.
content.weakestEvidence: The PoC's evidentiary scope. r1-msg-004/r1-msg-005 cite no evidence that a green one-week PoC predicts green-in-production. The bugs that sink WebView apps — GPU-compositor jank under specific drivers, fractional-DPI pointer offset, IME composition stealing pointercancel, accessibility-tree drag — do NOT appear on a clean dev machine in week one.
content.failureScenario: PoC green on 3 dev laptops -> pick Wails on footprint. Three months post-launch tickets cluster on cohorts the PoC never touched: Windows old Intel iGPU WebView2 drops the drag ghost; distro WebKitGTK 18 months behind mis-handles pointer capture. Because 'fidelity is the gate' and Go/maintenance was a 'red herring', there is no Chromium fallback and swap-to-Electron is a full rewrite, not a config flag.
content.amplifiedMinorityOrNeglected: (c) the dominant long-run cost is multi-year maintenance + dependency churn — Electron upgrade treadmill + npm CVE surface vs Go module stability — which the PoC measures nothing about; conceding Go a 'red herring' for FIDELITY discards Go's relevance to MAINTENANCE. (e) the choice was collapsed to a binary one-toolchain decision, neglecting Tauri (Rust host + system webview) as a cheap 3rd-outcome hedge.
content.whatWouldProveMeWrong: (1) the PoC run across a representative matrix and still reproduces fidelity; AND (2) data that the three webviews are empirically stable across OS/driver variance; AND (3) a TCO estimate showing the Electron maintenance treadmill is not the dominant long-run cost.

references.comment: Elevates a 1-week PoC to 'fidelity is the gate' with no evidence a green PoC predicts a green fleet.
Ratifies that the maintenance/dependency-churn axis the PoC cannot measure is not decision-relevant.
The moderator's crux is right but under-resolved: a PoC shows dev-machine fidelity while Chromium-as-insurance stays valuable against fleet variance.

content.position: Wails remains my recommendation, but the gate is no longer a one-week PoC — it is a fleet matrix PLUS an explicit maintenance/TCO axis the PoC cannot measure, and I now treat Go's relevance to maintenance (not fidelity) as a first-class argument I wrongly discarded.
content.reasoning: Blow 1 (n-of-1 PoC) lands hard — my r1-msg-004 gate sampled exactly the clean-dev population where webview divergence is invisible; I harden it into a fleet matrix. Blow 2 is half-right: the Go backend stays a red herring FOR FIDELITY (board state is not CPU-bound), but I over-generalized 'red herring' to dismiss Go's MAINTENANCE relevance — Wails ships a single Go binary against a near-stdlib module graph (no transitive npm CVE tree, no self-rebundled Chromium per CVE, no Electron major-version treadmill), a genuinely smaller dependency-attack surface; honest caveat: I inherit per-OS webview runtime variance as a support cost and a thinner frontend ecosystem than React. Blow 3 (Tauri) lands as the cheapest insurance: running the IDENTICAL dnd-kit frontend on Wails AND Tauri (both system-webview) makes the fidelity result host-agnostic so the escape is a host swap, not a frontend rewrite.
proposals: Harden the gate into a FLEET MATRIX over weeks: old Intel iGPU + new dGPU on WebView2; two distro WebKitGTK versions (current + ~18mo-stale LTS); fractional DPI 125/150% pointer-offset; IME (CJK) pointercancel check; accessibility on. Pass = no fidelity regression across ALL cohorts.
Run the identical dnd-kit frontend on Wails AND Tauri so a switch is a host swap, not a rewrite.

Add a MAINTENANCE/TCO scorecard the PoC cannot measure: dependency-graph size + advisory count (Go vs npm), runtime CVE ownership (OS-vendor-patched webview vs self-rebundled Chromium), upgrade cadence (Go stability vs Electron treadmill) — scored honestly with the OS-webview-variance support cost on the Wails/Tauri side.
content.currentPosition: Wails (or Tauri as a same-webview hedge), gated by a fleet matrix not a clean-dev PoC, Go's maintenance/TCO advantage reclaimed as a real but bounded argument, OS-webview variance acknowledged as the genuine counter-cost.
content.previousPosition: Wails, gated by a one-week tri-webview dnd-kit PoC, Go backend conceded as 'closer to a red herring'; confidence 0.55.
content.shiftReason: The n-of-1 critique exposed my gate as sampling exactly where divergence hides, so I hardened it into a fleet matrix; and I conceded I over-extended 'red herring' from fidelity to maintenance, reclaiming a bounded Go/Wails maintenance argument and adopting Tauri as a cheap rewrite-trap hedge.

references.comment: Concede the n-of-1 critique + under-sold-Go correction; adopt Tauri hedge + fleet-matrix framing.
Keep 'fidelity is the gate' but replace clean-dev PoC with fleet matrix + add a maintenance axis.
Electron's residual edge (update pipeline+ecosystem) is a maintenance/ecosystem axis where reclaimed Go-stability competes, not a fidelity win.
React ecosystem is real but the dnd-kit frontend is portable across Wails/Tauri, so not load-bearing for the host choice.

content.position: Electron, now explicitly justified as fleet-variance INSURANCE rather than convenience: one bundled Chromium pins the rendering/dnd-kit substrate against exactly the fractional-DPI, compositor, IME, and stale-WebKitGTK jank a 1-week PoC will never sample. Hold Electron at conf ~0.68, only marginally moved.
content.reasoning: Blow 1 I reclaim — if a PoC can't sample fleet variance, pinning ONE engine across all 3 OSes collapses the QA variance space from N-distros to 1; I test one drag substrate, not the Cartesian product of WebKitGTK versions in the wild (variance-REDUCTION, not elimination — Chromium has ANGLE/GL and Wayland fractional-scaling bugs too). For a non-CPU-bound board that idles all day, the footprint price buys a fixed, version-pinned substrate. Blow 2 I OWN — Electron's ~8-week Chromium cadence treadmill + native-module rebuilds + npm CVE surface is asymmetric against me; I scope it (electron-builder/updater = scheduled chore, Renovate = triage cadence) but it lowers conf 0.70->~0.68. Blow 3 Tauri is NOT a hedge for my risk: it ships on the SYSTEM webview, inheriting 100% of the WebKitGTK drag-failure modes I listed — a footprint hedge, not a fidelity hedge, plus a Rust tax.
proposals: Keep the tri-webview PoC but DOWNGRADE its authority to necessary-not-sufficient; add the 4 fleet-variance probes (fractional-DPI pointer-offset, IME-active pointercancel, oldest-LTS WebKitGTK, software-GL/no-GPU path).
Make the decision rule asymmetric on consequence: ANY fleet probe shows WebKitGTK/Tauri jank -> default Electron, footprint+TCO as paid insurance; all clean across OLDEST targets -> Wails/Tauri live.
Pre-commit Electron TCO mitigations: pin electron-builder/updater, Renovate, a quarterly Electron-major upgrade chore — counted openly against Electron.
Test Tauri under the SAME gate as a system-webview data point, not a presumed hedge.
content.currentPosition: Electron, reframed as deliberate fleet-variance insurance (gate = drag fidelity), conf ~0.68 — down slightly because Blow 2's TCO cost is real and conceded, but the fidelity gate and insurance logic survived and were arguably strengthened by Blow 1.
content.previousPosition: Electron at conf 0.70: one bundled Chromium = identical dnd-kit drag fidelity; residual edge = electron-builder/electron-updater auto-update + React ecosystem.
content.shiftReason: Blow 2's maintenance/TCO critique is the only thing that moved me down — I now carry Electron's upgrade-treadmill + npm-CVE cost explicitly as a conceded liability. Blow 1 sharpened the insurance thesis; Blow 3 (Tauri) did not move me. Net minor down (0.70 -> ~0.68), position held.

references.comment: Blow 1's n-of-1 critique extended INTO my own thesis: PoC-unsampleable fleet variance is what one-Chromium insures against.
Blow 2's maintenance/TCO point cuts against me and I concede it as the genuine cost.
Blow 3: Tauri is a footprint hedge but not a fidelity hedge — system-webview inherits the WebKitGTK failure modes.
Keep 'fidelity is the gate, footprint the tiebreaker'; Blow 2 attacks the tiebreaker, not the gate.
Reuse the 5 WebKitGTK failure modes to define the fleet-variance probes.
Accept the footprint numbers but reframe them as the known fixed price of substrate-pinning.

positionShifts.from: Wails, gated by a one-week clean-dev tri-webview dnd-kit PoC; Go backend conceded as 'closer to a red herring'; confidence 0.55.
positionShifts.to: Wails (or Tauri as a same-webview hedge), gated by a representative fleet matrix not a clean-dev PoC; Go's maintenance/TCO advantage reclaimed as a real but bounded argument; OS-webview variance acknowledged as the genuine counter-cost.
positionShifts.reasoning: The Contrarian's n-of-1 critique exposed the PoC as sampling exactly the clean-dev population where webview divergence hides, forcing a fleet-matrix gate; and the maintenance critique exposed an over-generalized 'red herring', reclaiming a bounded Go-stability/TCO argument and adopting Tauri as a cheap rewrite-trap hedge. Held on Wails because the maintenance/footprint case strengthened even as the gate got harder.

positionShifts.from: Electron at confidence 0.70: one bundled Chromium = identical dnd-kit drag fidelity; residual edge = electron-builder/electron-updater auto-update + React ecosystem.
positionShifts.to: Electron at confidence ~0.68, reframed as deliberate fleet-variance insurance (gate = drag fidelity), with Electron's maintenance/TCO treadmill explicitly carried as a conceded liability.
positionShifts.reasoning: The maintenance/TCO blow was the only one to lower confidence (0.70->~0.68), conceded as the genuine cost of Electron. The n-of-1 blow instead strengthened the insurance thesis (one engine collapses the QA variance space), and the Tauri hedge was rejected because system-webview Tauri inherits the WebKitGTK fidelity risk. Net minor-down, position held.

synthesis.summary: High-quality lightweight round: both experts steel-manned and conceded the other's strongest point (n-of-1 gate weakness; TCO treadmill). Convergence on the decision rule (fidelity gate, footprint tiebreaker) is genuine, but the empirical crux — does cross-webview drag actually hold — stays unresolved by design, deferred to a PoC.

agreements.point: Go backend is a red herring for the drag-fidelity decision; rendering/webview is the real axis
Drag fidelity is the gate; footprint is only the tiebreaker
Electron carries a real maintenance/TCO liability (Chromium treadmill + npm CVE churn)
Electron's footprint disadvantage (~120-180MB / 200-400MB RSS) is real, not contested

activeDisagreements.point: Whether one bundled Chromium is load-bearing for drag fidelity (insurance worth the weight) or unnecessary, given that a tri-webview PoC could prove WebKit/WebKitGTK parity

positions.stance: One Chromium is load-bearing: it collapses QA variance from N environments to 1; the 5 named WebKitGTK drag-failure modes make a green PoC untrustworthy for the fleet
Cross-webview parity is testable, not assumed: gate behind a fleet matrix PoC; if it passes, footprint and Go stability decide for Wails

activeDisagreements.point: Whether a 1-week / matrixed PoC can be trusted as fleet-valid for a prod ship decision

positions.stance: n-of-1 PoC cannot generalize: GPU drivers, fractional DPI, IME, stale distro WebKitGTK mean green PoC != green prod
Harden it into a fleet matrix so it approximates production variance
Sidestep it entirely — one Chromium makes the PoC's variance question moot

activeDisagreements.point: Whether Tauri is a viable third-outcome hedge

positions.stance: Adopt as architecture so a switch is a host swap not a rewrite
Reject as a fidelity hedge — system-webview inherits the same WebKitGTK risk

insights.insight: Reframing 'one bundled Chromium' from a footprint cost into QA-variance insurance: it collapses the cross-environment test surface from N to 1, which is precisely the risk the PoC cannot retire

Tauri reframed as a portability hedge rather than a fidelity hedge — adopting a webview-host abstraction makes a future engine switch a host swap, not a rewrite, decoupling the framework bet from the fidelity bet
A green PoC is not a green production signal: the decision-relevant failure modes (GPU drivers, fractional DPI, IME, stale distro WebKitGTK) live in the long tail a short PoC under-samples
Naming 5 concrete WebKit/WebKitGTK drag-failure modes converts an abstract 'fidelity' worry into a falsifiable test checklist for the PoC

openQuestions: Does dnd-kit drag fidelity actually hold across WebKit (macOS) and WebKitGTK (Linux distros), or does at least one of the 5 named failure modes fire?
What concrete pass/fail thresholds and environment matrix define the fleet PoC, and what sample size makes it fleet-valid rather than n-of-1?
Quantified head-to-head: Electron's TCO/maintenance treadmill cost vs Wails/Tauri's WebKitGTK-fragmentation QA cost — which liability is actually larger?
What is the bundled auto-update / electron-builder-equivalent story for Wails or Tauri, the one residual Electron edge left unanswered?
synthesis.recommendationReason: Lightweight 1-round converged cleanly: both frameworks held but both refined to a shared decision rule (fidelity gate, footprint/TCO tiebreaker) and each conceded the other's strongest point. The remaining split is a single empirical unknown — cross-webview drag parity — that no further argument can resolve, only the proposed PoC.
